elements.sent-2411-001: Asymptotic properties of classical field electrodynamics are considered.
elements.sent-2411-002: Special attention is paid to the long-range structure of the electromagnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-003: It is shown that conserved Poincar\'e quantities may be expressed in terms of the asymptotic fields.
elements.sent-2411-004: Long-range variables are shown to be responsible for an angular momentum contribution which mixes Coulomb and infrared free field characteristics; otherwise angular momentum and energy-momentum separate into electromagnetic and matter fields contributions.
elements.sent-2411-005: It is well-known that the long-range character of electromagnetic field causes certain peculiarities in quantum electrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-006: Among them the infraparticle problem and breaking of the Lorentz symmetry are the most spectacular ones, for a review see the book by Haag \cite{haa and an article by Morchio and Strochci \cite{mor .}}
elements.sent-2411-007: These properties can be traced back, as shown most clearly by Buchholz \cite{buc86 , to the fact provable within the standard system of ideas on properties of quantum electrodynamics that the flux of electromagnetic field at spacelike infinity is an essentially classical variable supplying a label for uncountably many superselection sectors \cite{buc82 .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Whether these are ultimate features of the quantum theory of electromagnetic interaction or artifacts due to our insufficient understanding of its algebraic structure is in our opinion an open question as long as we lack consistent, complete QED beyond Feynman graphs.
elements.sent-2411-009: Doubts about completeness of the present-state knowledge of the long-range structure can also be raised on grounds that it tells nothing about the quantization of charge or the magnitude of the fine-structure constant; see works by Staruszkiewicz on this point \cite{sta89,sta92 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In the present work we try to better understand the long-range structure of electrodynamics in classical field theory.
elements.sent-2411-011: We believe that in this way one can gain new insights into the quantum case as well.
elements.sent-2411-012: The domain in which the classical structure is most likely to be of some relevance for the quantum case is the asymptotic region.
elements.sent-2411-013: Rigorous results on the asymptotics of electromagnetic field are presented in Section \ref{selm and on the asymptotics of Dirac field in Section \ref{sdir .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The results are relevant for the interacting theory, as argued in Sections \ref{selm and \ref{stot .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In that case some additional assumptions are made which seem plausible, but remain unproved.
elements.sent-2411-016: Our main objective, when discussing the asymptotic fields, is the description of the specific way how matter and radiation separate in the asymptotic regions.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this respect the approach of the present paper differs from that of Flato, Simon and Taflin, who have recently reported rigorous results on Cauchy problem and scattering states in classical Maxwell-Dirac theory \cite{fla ; see also a comment in Section \ref{stot .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Using results on asymptotic fields we express energy-momentum and angular momentum of the system in terms of those fields.
elements.sent-2411-019: We stress that our aim is not a purely mathematical study in classical field theory.
elements.sent-2411-020: Rather, with quantization in mind, we try to get a reasonably well-founded notion of the asymptotic structure of fields and conserved Poincar\'e-quantities.
elements.sent-2411-021: Some of the results described in the present work were reported earlier in a letter \cite{her93 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Quantization of the long-range variables within this approach in a kind of "adiabatic approximation" was discussed in \cite{her .}

elements.section-2411-002: Null asymptotics of the electromagnetic field
elements.sent-2411-031: In this section we describe some null asymptotic properties of the electromagnetic fields.
elements.sent-2411-032: Much of the material is not new, the null infinity methods being the standard tool in the relativity theory.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, we do not use the Penrose's conformal compactification, as employed in similar context in \cite{bra and \cite{ash , and use an explicitly Lorentz-covariant description in terms of homogeneous functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Moreover, we describe some global properties in Minkowski space, which are needed in the discussion of Lorentz generators.
elements.sent-2411-035: The reason for avoiding the conformal compactification is that it contracts the timelike past and future infinity to points.
elements.sent-2411-036: This does not seem a natural setting for the description of massive asymptotic fields living there, which is our concern in Section \ref{sdir .}

elements.sent-2411-382: The main results of our analysis can be summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-383: Despite nonintegrability of the angular momentum tensor density over a Cauchy surface, the total angular momentum (four dimensional) can be unambiguously identified, provided (a) angular momentum radiated (or incoming from null directions) over finite time intervals is well defined, and (b) the magnetic part of the spacelike asymptotic of the electromagnetic field vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-384: Asymptotic Dirac field can be identified by a special choice of gauge and consideration of the asymptotic behavior of the Dirac field on the hyperboloid $x^2=\lambda^2$ for $\lambda\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-385: The total energy momentum of the system can be expressed as a sum of independent contributions from the asymptotic free electromagnetic field and the asymptotic Dirac field.
elements.sent-2411-386: However, in the analogous representation of the angular momentum an additional term survives, which mixes the asymptotic Dirac field characteristic with the infrared characteristic of the free asymptotic electromagnetic field.

elements.sent-2411-393: We reproduce here some facts about the invariant measure over the null directions in Minkowski space \cite{sta,zwa,pen and on spin-weighted spherical harmonics \cite{pen .}}
elements.sent-2411-394: Let $u$ denote a vector on the future lightcone.
elements.sent-2411-395: The measure $\dsp {d^3 u\over u^0}$ is known to be Lorentz invariant.
elements.sent-2411-396: If we define a measure over the null directions $d^2u$ by $\dsp {d^3 u\over u^0 = {du^0\over u^0 d^2u$ (in the notation of \cite{sta ), then the new measure is Lorentz invariant in the following sense: The result of integration of a homogeneous of degree $-2$ function of $u$ is manifestly Lorentz invariant.}}}
elements.sent-2411-397: The invariant measure has a very simple and elegant representation in the spinor language \cite{pen .}
elements.sent-2411-398: If $\xi^A$ is a spinor of the null vector $u$, then \begin{equation}d^2u = i\xi^{A'} d\xi_{A'}\wedge \xi^A d\xi_A \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-399: Here any parametrization of spinors is implied for which every null direction is represented by exactly one spinor.

elements.sent-2411-401: Some special scalings of spinors are useful.
elements.sent-2411-402: We say that a spinor $o^A$ is chosen in a $t$-gauge, if its null vector $l$ satisfies $t\s l =1$, where $t$ is a~fixed unit timelike vector.

elements.formula-2411-090: \begin{equation}(2\pi)^2\int_{-r}^r \ov{f_1(s, o, \oc)} f_2(s, o, \oc)\, ds +2\int_{-r}^r ds\int_{0}^1 \frac{d\kappa}{\sqrt{1-\kappa^2}}\int d\theta\,d\f\, \D_{\theta}\lp\cos\frac{\theta}{2}\ov{f_1(s, \xi, \xc)}\rp \times [1ex] \times\lb f_2(s\cos\theta+\sqrt{r^2-s^2}\kappa\sin\theta, o, \oc) + f_2(s\cos\theta-\sqrt{r^2-s^2}\kappa\sin\theta, o, \oc)\rb\, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-448: By the Lebesgue theorem the second integral vanishes in the limit, which ends the proof.
elements.sent-2411-449: We show here, that if $2\geq\beta\geq 0 $, $\g\geq 0$, $\alpha>|\beta+\g -1|$ and $\dsp |G(z, v)|<\frac{\con {(v^0)^{\alpha +1 }$ for both $z$ and $v$ on the unit four-velocity hyperboloid, then \be \left|\int\frac{G(z, v) {\lp\sqrt{(z\s v)^2 -1}\rp^\beta \lp z\s v + \sqrt{(z\s v)^2 -1}\rp^\g}\, \m(v)\right| < \frac{\con {(z^0)^{\beta+\g }\, .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-450: Let $t^a$ be the time-axis versor of the reference system.
elements.sent-2411-451: We show that \begin{equation}I\equiv\int\frac{\m(v)}{\lp\sqrt{(z\s v)^2 -1}\rp^\beta \lp z\s v + \sqrt{(z\s v)^2 -1}\rp^\g (t\s v)^{\alpha +1}} < \frac{\con}{(t\s z)^{\beta+\g}}\, .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-091: \begin{equation}I\equiv\int\frac{\m(v)}{\lp\sqrt{(z\s v)^2 -1}\rp^\beta \lp z\s v + \sqrt{(z\s v)^2 -1}\rp^\g (t\s v)^{\alpha +1}} < \frac{\con}{(t\s z)^{\beta+\g}}\, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-452: Choose the time-axis of the coordinate system in which integration is performed along $z$ and set $v^0=\sqrt{|\vec{v |^2 +1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-454: By (\ref{est ) the inside integral is bounded by \begin{equation}\frac{\con}{(\cosh\ch v^0 - \sinh\ch |\vec{v}|)^\alpha (\cosh\ch v^0 + \sinh\ch |\vec{v}|)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-455: By change of integration variable $|\vec{v | = \sinh\psi$ we get \begin{equation}I<\con\int_0^\infty\frac{(\sinh\psi)^{2-\beta}\, d\psi}{(e^{\textstyle\psi})^\g \cosh(\psi+\ch)(\cosh(\psi-\ch))^\alpha} <\frac{\con}{(\cosh\ch)^{\beta+\g}}\int_{-\ch}^{\infty} \frac{d\psi}{(e^{\textstyle\psi})^{\beta+\g-1}(\cosh\psi)^\alpha} < \frac{\con}{(\cosh\ch)^{\beta+\g}}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{d\psi}{(e^{\textstyle\psi})^{\beta+\g-1}(\cosh\psi)^\alpha}\, .\end{equation}.}
